steps.explanation: Compute $f(x+1)$ and compare it to $f(x)$: $$f(x+1) = 66b^{x+1} = 66 \cdot b^x \cdot b = b \cdot f(x)$$ So increasing $x$ by $1$ multiplies the output by $b$.
The percentage increase from $f(x)$ to $b \cdot f(x)$ is: $$\frac{b \cdot f(x) - f(x)}{f(x)} \times 100 = (b - 1) \times 100$$ Therefore $c = 100(b - 1)$.
Since $0 \lt c \lt 100$, we need $0 \lt 100(b-1) \lt 100$, which means $1 \lt b \lt 2$. This confirms $b$ is a growth factor slightly above $1$, consistent with the constraint.
choices: • Choice A: $1 + \frac{b}{100}$ — this expression doesn't arise from the percentage-increase formula. Eliminated.
• Choice B: $b + 100$ — adding $100$ to $b$ would give a value well above $100$ for any positive $b$, violating $c \lt 100$. Eliminated.
• Choice C: $100(b + 1)$ — this adds $1$ instead of subtracting; it overstates the increase. Eliminated.
• Choice D: $100(b - 1)$ — derived directly from the ratio $\frac{f(x+1) - f(x)}{f(x)} \times 100 = (b-1) \times 100$. That matches choice D — D is correct.
